name: Monkshood
danger: Potentially fatal — avoid even skin contact
notes: Tops the RHS danger list. Ingestion is nearly always fatal.

name: Foxglove
danger: Cardiac glycosides in all parts including pollen
notes: One of the UK's most commonly grown plants and wildflowers.

name: Yew
danger: All parts lethal except fleshy red aril — seeds inside aril are toxic
notes: Ubiquitous in Surrey hedging and churchyards.

name: Lily of the Valley
danger: Consuming just two leaves can be fatal — attractive red berries appeal to children
notes: Produces attractive red berries that appeal to children.

name: Deadly Nightshade
notes: Found wild in some parts of southern England.

name: Castor Oil Plant
danger: Seeds contain ricin — one of the most toxic natural substances
notes: Sometimes grown as an ornamental annual.

name: Laburnum
danger: Seed pods resemble pea pods and strongly attract children
notes: A very common Surrey garden tree.

name: Euphorbia
danger: Milky sap causes skin blistering and eye damage

name: Giant Hogweed
danger: Phototoxic sap causes severe burns on contact with skin in sunlight
notes: Found along Surrey waterways. A notifiable invasive species.

name: Lords-and-Ladies
danger: Bright red berries and burning sap — self-seeds into gardens from hedgerows
notes: Self-seeds into Surrey gardens from hedgerows.

name: Oleander
danger: All parts highly toxic — even smoke from burning is dangerous
notes: More common in Mediterranean climates but grown under cover in UK.

name: Angel's Trumpet
notes: Grown as a conservatory plant in the UK.

name: Autumn Crocus
danger: Contains colchicine — toxic in small amounts
notes: Found in meadows and sometimes in garden borders.
